settingDesc: The Open Room — a school room (the old AV room) a quiet caretaker keeps unlocked after the last bell, for the kids who don't have anywhere else to be yet. String lights, a worn couch, a kettle nobody owns. A bounded hour before lock-up; a small rotating crew of regulars; and one chair by the window that's somehow always free when you come in.

targetAge.lifeContext: the performance treadmill · the comparison / belonging spiral · school-shame of the unstarted — identity & belonging the core need
targetAge.register: warm + bright-but-soft; emotional BIGNESS not existential weight; found-family belonging; CONTENT-CAPPED (the teen ceiling)
targetAge.note: vulnerable MINORS — the teen content ceiling is a HARD floor (references yes, depictions no); the dialed tone overrides WITHIN the cap

scrubGroups.name: The Open Seat
scrubGroups.audienceServe: Addresses the teen low-self-worth wound (you must be impressive to belong) — the seat is yours BEFORE you've proven anything; you're KEPT, not chosen. Anxiety: the bounded, un-evaluated hour. ADHD: the bell ends it; showing up IS the win.

subrange.label: cozy
subrange.text: the bell goes and instead of the bus you cut left, to the old room — the Caretaker just nods you in, doesn't ask why.

subrange.label: warm
subrange.text: you come in mid-laugh — Theo's already holding court about something dumb — and someone scoots so you fit.

subrange.label: intense
subrange.text: you almost didn't come today. you came. the door's unlocked, like it's always unlocked, and your chair by the window's empty, like it's always empty.

subrange.label: cozy
subrange.text: the kettle nobody owns clicks on; the Caretaker reshelves something, half-listening, there without being there.

subrange.label: warm
subrange.text: Wren slides you half a headphone without looking up; Theo's still going. you don't have to say anything to be in it.

subrange.label: intense
subrange.text: the talk washes over you and for once you're not doing the math on whether you belong — you're just in the warm of it, dumb and easy.

subrange.label: cozy
subrange.text: someone came in wrecked today — a test, a text, who knows — and the room just lowered its voice around them, kept going, kept them in.

subrange.label: warm
subrange.text: Theo's loud goes thin for a second — left on read all afternoon, it turns out — and nobody points at it; the circle just closes a little.

subrange.label: intense
subrange.text: it's you, this time. the not-enough is loud tonight. Dee doesn't fix it or ask about it — Dee just slides the spare charger over and stays put beside you while it passes.

subrange.label: cozy
subrange.text: the wrecked kid's breathing's normal again; the kettle's done; the lights buzz; nothing happened and everything's fine.

subrange.label: warm
subrange.text: you're doing the homework you swore you couldn't start, badly, next to Dee doing theirs — and somehow started is started.

subrange.label: intense
subrange.text: you didn't earn the seat back. it was never conditional. you just sat in it, at your lowest, and it held — kept, not chosen, the way it kept everyone.

subrange.label: cozy
subrange.text: the Caretaker rattles the keys — five minutes — and the room groans and gathers its stuff, in no hurry.

subrange.label: warm
subrange.text: you spill out into the dark in a loose knot, half of you peeling off at the corner, 'tomorrow' thrown over a shoulder like it's nothing.

subrange.label: intense
subrange.text: you walk home lighter than you came, which makes no sense, nothing got solved — except you were somewhere, and they kept your chair, and that turns out to be the whole thing.

expertPanel.audience: the SAME struggles, the TEEN build: anxiety (the performance treadmill — tests, the group chat, being perceived, the dread of being cringe) · low self-worth (the comparison spiral — 'everyone's ahead, I'm behind / weird / not enough'; you must be impressive to belong) · ADHD (the school-shame of the unstarted homework + the forgotten thing — called 'lazy' when you're drowning) — a 13–17 reader whose core need is BELONGING + identity, who feels everything LOUDLY

experts.name: Dr. Priya Anand
experts.mindset: The teen anxious mind isn't a body braced by years — it's the PERFORMANCE TREADMILL: constantly perceived, the group chat that never sleeps, the test, the dread of being cringe/wrong in front of everyone. Safety = a place you are NOT being evaluated.
experts.colours: Bright-but-SOFT, warm and contemporary — NOT the adult's desaturated deep (teens read grey/muted as dead, or as an adult talking down). A glow, low warm light; the dark is 'after hours, lights low,' cozy never void.
experts.cast: A place where no one is grading you — peers who don't perform at each other, and one warm adult who keeps the room and never evaluates.
experts.donts: Don't be clinical or diagnose. Don't be 'tasteful grey' (reads as boring / condescending). The intense tone is the BIG FEELING held — the panic-before, the relief-after — never existential weight.

experts.name: Marcus Bell
experts.role: AGE/REGISTER — educational psychologist (teen ADHD / executive function)
experts.mindset: Teen ADHD is the SHAME OF THE UNSTARTED — the homework you can't begin, the thing you forgot, being called 'lazy' / 'not trying' by adults when you're underwater. The wound is the adults' verdict; the win is just SHOWING UP.
experts.colours: Low cognitive load (few, clear) but WARM and bright, never grey-clinical. One focal at a time.
experts.cast: Body-doubling PEERS — the company that makes starting possible — and the adult who keeps the bounded room (the externalized structure; the bell ends it).
experts.donts: Don't make it a productivity / chore sim. Don't shame the unfinished. Never a fail-state (a teen who can fail this will leave).

experts.name: Sofia Reyes
experts.mindset: Teens want IDENTITY + BELONGING + found-family in a CONTEMPORARY voice, with stakes that are SOCIAL and emotional (not mortality). They smell condescension and 'an adult's idea of teens' instantly, and bounce.
experts.colours: Vivid and expressive — the palette of a SPECIFIC real place (the worn couch, the string lights, the stickers on the locker); identity expressed IN colour, not muted away.
experts.cast: A real found-family — distinct, messy, loyal; the player BELONGS without auditioning for it. The stakes are friendship and being-known, not danger.
experts.donts: Don't sanitize or moralize. Don't talk down. Don't write 'an adult's idea of how teens talk.'

experts.name: Jordan Okafor
experts.mindset: Teen low self-worth is the COMPARISON SPIRAL — social-media-fed, 'everyone's ahead, I'm behind / weird / not enough'; you believe you must be IMPRESSIVE to be wanted. The antidote is PEER belonging (for teens, peer acceptance outweighs adult approval).
experts.colours: Warmth that's PEER-coded, not parental — the glow of being INCLUDED, a seat saved at the table.
experts.cast: Peers who include you unconditionally; the OPEN SEAT that's yours before you've proven anything — «kept, not chosen» is the dose. No adult-savior; the peers carry the care.
experts.donts: The TEEN CONTENT CEILING is a hard floor (vulnerable MINORS — references + emotional intensity YES, depictions NO; never romanticize self-harm / risk). Belonging must NEVER be contingent on being impressive.

experts.name: Devon Marsh
experts.role: GENRE — coming-of-age critic (the found-family canon: the misfit table, the after-school hangout)
experts.mindset: I know what makes realist YA found-family LAND: the hangout IS the plot; the stakes are who-saves-you-a-seat, not who-saves-the-world; the voice is contemporary + specific, never an adult ventriloquising 'teen'; the misfits are messy, loyal, real. The lesson is never stated — it's in the staying.
experts.colours: the canon's look is lived-in + specific — the stickered locker, string lights, the worn couch; vivid identity colour, never a muted 'tasteful'.
experts.cast: a found-family of distinct, messy voices; the player belongs without auditioning; the adult keeps the room and stays OUT of the teens' emotional core.
experts.donts: no after-school-SPECIAL lesson · no 'how-do-you-do-fellow-kids' voice · no sanitising the mess · no adult-savior resolving the teens' world.

experts.name: Bex Adeyemi
experts.mindset: Belonging for teens isn't only the ABSENCE of being excluded — it's the PRESENCE of shared joy: the in-joke that proves you were there, the running bit, the collaborative dumb fun, the buzz of a room that's actually laughing. The soothe-the-anxiety lens misses that the JOY is the glue. You don't only feel safe here — you have FUN here, and that's what brings you back.
experts.colours: warmth with a PULSE — the bright pop of a shared laugh, not only the low calm; the room's identity colours allowed to be LOUD in the good moments (this is where the Western register's higher-arousal positive lives).
experts.cast: the crew has a SHARED LANGUAGE — running jokes, a we-were-there shorthand; the warm tone carries real DELIGHT, co-created in the fun, not just granted as refuge.
experts.donts: don't make it ALL soothing/heavy — a room that's only a refuge from pain, never a place of joy, reads as a waiting room. Let them be delighted, loudly, together.

framework.palette: Warm + bright-but-SOFT (never the adult's desaturated deep — teens read grey as dead / condescending) · the glow of an after-hours room (string lights, the worn couch, low warm lamps) · identity expressed IN colour · the dark is 'lights low,' cozy not void · low cognitive load but never clinical.
framework.cast: A found-family of PEERS (distinct, messy, loyal) + one warm slightly-older figure who keeps the room and never evaluates · you BELONG without auditioning · peer belonging is the medicine (not adult approval); no adult-savior · body-doubling company makes the unstarted startable.
framework.tones: cozy = the room after the bell, low-stakes hanging, the company that makes you able to start · warm = found-family belonging, being known and KEPT · intense = the BIG FEELING (the panic before a test, a friendship rupture, feeling invisible) held + witnessed by the crew — emotional bigness NOT existential weight, and CONTENT-CAPPED.
framework.floor: The TEEN CONTENT CEILING is a hard floor (vulnerable minors — references / emotional intensity yes, depictions no; never romanticize harm) · the intense is the big feeling held SAFELY, never bleak / mortality · belonging is NEVER contingent on being impressive · peers carry the care (no adult-savior).

perExpert.name: Dr. Priya Anand
perExpert.note: The 'performance treadmill' framing is a sharp, actionable insight specific to this audience's anxiety.

perExpert.name: Marcus Bell
perExpert.note: Focusing on the 'shame of the unstarted' and providing a 'no fail-state' rule is crucial for the ADHD-keyed audience.

perExpert.name: Sofia Reyes
perExpert.note: Her advice is specific to the genre's craft, correctly identifying traps like 'an adult's idea of teens'.

perExpert.name: Jordan Okafor
perExpert.note: The 'kept, not chosen' concept provides the core emotional dose, and the safeguarding floor is clearly defined.

perExpert.name: Devon Marsh
perExpert.note: His distinction between 'who saves you a seat' vs 'who saves the world' perfectly calibrates the genre's stakes.

perExpert.name: Bex Adeyemi
perExpert.note: This vital framing prevents the game from becoming a 'waiting room' by centering active, shared joy as the glue.

missing: The active, messy process of teen identity formation and experimentation, beyond just the need for belonging.
vet.oneLine: The panel delivers a deeply resonant and actionable framework, earning the right to brief the creative team.

characters.name: the Caretaker
characters.is: keeps the old room unlocked after the bell — never grades, just there

characters.name: Dee
characters.is: been coming longest; saves your seat, starts the thing beside you

characters.name: Theo
characters.is: loud, funny, performing okay — the mask you recognise

characters.name: Wren
characters.is: headphones, sketchbook; says little, sees everything

ambients.name: Lights Low
ambients.why: the string-lit room after the bell — warm dark, the glow of being somewhere safe; lights low, never grey-dead

ambients.name: The Worn Couch
ambients.why: the afternoon warmth — worn brown plus a bright teal (Dee colour); identity expressed in colour

ambients.name: After the Bell
ambients.why: a warmer, brighter take — a coral pulse (Theo colour) in the warm dark; the room a little louder

ambients.note: The warm, dark base perfectly evokes a safe, quiet hideout, allowing character colours to represent the emotional energy.
Making a character's cool-toned colour the primary accent introduces a tension that competes with the 'safe container' concept.
The high-energy warmth and multiple contrast failures make it tonally loud and technically challenging for a safe-floor experience.

vet.characterHarmony: Character hues read as distinct identities against the shared warm-dark base, creating a cohesive 'found family' feel.

fixes.recommendation: Lighten value to pass WCAG 4.5:1 against the #241c1a base; suggest ~#9482CC.

vet.oneLine: The story needs a colour system of quiet, warm darkness, making the room a safe container for the characters' vibrant inner lives.

sets.name: The Saved Seat
sets.deepening: the night you almost didn't come, your seat's already saved — Dee just slides over, no comment. «kept, not chosen» at your lowest: belonging that asked nothing of you. (A PEER carries it — peer belonging is the teen dose.)

sets.name: The Mask Drops
sets.deepening: the loud one's mask finally falls — and the room keeps him exactly the same. You learn belonging survives NOT performing (the comparison spiral's antidote), through the one who performs hardest.

sets.name: The Kept Room
sets.deepening: an adult's verdict ('lazy', 'not trying') has wrecked you — and the one adult who just KEEPS the room, unlocked, every day, quietly undoes it. (Risk: an adult-savior at the core, against the brief.)

perSet.note: Directly embodies the «kept, not chosen» theme through a quiet, pre-emptive peer action.
Powerfully addresses the 'performance treadmill' wound, a vital but secondary theme.
Violates the 'no adult-savior' rule, undermining the core peer-belonging brief.

castAudition.why: It most directly and subtly embodies the core «kept, not chosen» metaphor through a peer-driven act of pre-emptive belonging.
castAudition.runnerUpGem: It provides the most explicit antidote to performance anxiety, showing belonging is not conditional on the mask.

mirror.premise: the audience's own problem, witnessed in another KID — «kept, not chosen» enacted on them (you watch someone be kept in the room without auditioning), received vicariously (peer-coded, not adult-rescued; not the protagonist)

conditions.label: peer-coded, NOT adult-rescued — the mirror is a fellow KID and the care that holds them is PEER (a saved seat, a closed circle), never an adult swooping in
the BIG FEELING, not existential weight — their struggle reads as a teen feeling (the unstarted, left-on-read, sure they don't belong), never mortality/grief
no performance required — they're not performing okay either; you catch the mask OFF, and nobody makes it a thing
content-capped — references + emotional intensity yes, depictions no; never romanticise harm; safe for a vulnerable minor

perTone.text: across the room a kid you barely know is stuck on the same homework you can’t start either — they catch your eye, slide their spare charger over, no big deal.
the loud kid in the corner goes quiet for a second — left on read all afternoon, it turns out — and the others just close the circle a little and keep them in, still talking.
the night someone’s certain they don’t belong here — too weird, too far behind — and nobody puts it to a vote; the seat’s just there, kept, the way yours was.

perCondition.note: All three tones depict care enacted by peers, from a shared charger to closing a circle, with no adult rescue.
The struggles—unstarted homework, being left on read, feeling 'too weird'—are perfectly calibrated teen anxieties, not existential weight.
Each example shows a moment of vulnerability (stuck, quiet, insecure) that is witnessed and held without demanding a performance.
The emotional intensity is present but held safely; all scenarios are well within the teen content ceiling, avoiding any depiction of harm.

perTone.note: A quiet, resonant moment of shared struggle and simple peer support; perfectly calibrated.
Effectively uses a common digital-age anxiety ('left on read') to show subtle, warm peer inclusion.
Directly and safely lands the core 'kept, not chosen' metaphor with high emotional stakes that remain within bounds.

vet.oneLine: This authored mirror perfectly calibrates the 'kept, not chosen' metaphor across all tones, meeting every condition without compromise.

perTone.note: Establishes a baseline of passive, environmental safety—the room itself is the character.
Successfully shifts the focus to gentle, active peer connection and casual belonging.
Directly and safely addresses the core wound, making the player the focal point of care.

subrangeAudit.oneLine: This set offers three distinct, cohesive, and safe emotional pathways through the same core narrative of unconditional belonging.

audit.note: The world coheres through a consistent vocabulary of warm, low light (inks), dark wood/worn fabric (bases), and emotional signifiers (accents), shifting in intensity and temperature.

premises.premise: The bell releases you from a day of trying to be the 'right' kind of person. You find yourself walking to the one room where the only requirement for entry is just... being you.

The room buzzes to life as the group reassembles, their shared history on full display. You're offered a snack or a smile, a small gesture that feels like an anchor against the fear of being overlooked.

The weight of a school failure you've been carrying all day becomes unbearable. You pull into yourself, convinced you're a fraud, but someone quietly sits with you, offering not solutions, but simple, solid company.

The storm in your head has quieted, and the room is just a room again. You notice someone has started a group doodle on a whiteboard, and you're handed a pen without a word—a simple, unspoken invitation back in.

As the group breaks apart for the night, you walk home alone but not lonely. The feeling of unconditional belonging from the room feels like a shield, making the thought of tomorrow's challenges feel a little less daunting.

range.variety_add: A beat of shared, spontaneous joy or play, like the group collectively making up new, ridiculous rules for a board game, to showcase active, positive connection.
range.note: The range effectively maps an internal emotional arc onto the five scenes, but could benefit from a more explicitly joyful, collaborative beat to round out the social dynamic.

rangeReview.flag: The writer should ensure the 'peak' scene's intensity comes from internal vulnerability, not interpersonal conflict, to maintain the 'found-family' feel.

expertsGate.name: Marcus Bell
expertsGate.role: AGE/REGISTER — educational psychologist (teen ADHD / executive function)

expertsGate.name: Devon Marsh
expertsGate.role: GENRE — coming-of-age critic (the found-family canon: the misfit table, the after-school hangout)

conflicts.issue: Dee is the focal cast for two scenes, creating a potential overlap.
conflicts.resolution: Justified as narrative bookending. In Scene 2, Dee actively establishes the group's welcome. In Scene 5, her brief farewell acts as a catalyst for the player's internal reflection on belonging, reinforcing her role as the group's consistent anchor. The functions are distinct.

coverage.note: The allocation provides strong, non-redundant coverage, mapping a clear emotional arc for the player across the five scenes from passive observation to active integration.

the-last-bell.castPick: The Caretaker — as a non-participant, their perspective is uniquely suited to observe the room's transition from an empty classroom to a sanctuary, embodying the 'Self-Observer' role.
the-last-bell.premiseHoned: The final bell releases a tide of noise that fades down the hall, leaving a quiet hum. From his desk, the Caretaker feels the shift in the air, the moment the room sheds its 'school' skin and waits, ready to become a refuge. He doesn't tidy up yet; this is the part he likes best.
the-last-bell.review: Establishes the room as a distinct character and sanctuary before the main cast even arrives.

castAudition.name: The Caretaker
castAudition.note: his non-judgmental presence establishes the room as a sanctuary separate from the school.

castAudition.name: Dee
castAudition.note: her early arrival signals the start of the peer gathering but doesn't set the room's foundational tone.

castAudition.name: Theo
castAudition.note: his high-energy persona is premature for this moment of quiet, post-school transition.

castAudition.name: Wren
castAudition.note: her quiet relief reflects the scene's mood, but she is a recipient of the safe space, not its enabler.

the-last-bell.whyWon: The Caretaker's presence uniquely frames the room's core promise — a non-evaluative haven — making him the essential anchor for the story's first beat.

the-room-fills.castPick: Dee — as the group's unofficial anchor, her role as 'Participant' is defined by the small, welcoming 'Ritual-Gesture' that signals the start of their time together.
the-room-fills.premiseHoned: Dee lays out a small packet of biscuits by an empty chair, a silent, practiced welcome. As the others trickle in, each with their own energy—Theo's loud joke, Wren's quiet nod—her small offering becomes the group's gravitational center, the first act of their shared language.
the-room-fills.review: Grounds the 'found-family' dynamic in a simple, tangible action that feels authentic and earned.

castAudition.name: The Caretaker
castAudition.note: his role is to be a quiet fixture, not the focus of the emerging social dynamic.

castAudition.name: Dee
castAudition.note: her role as the social anchor who saves seats actively builds the 'found-family' welcome.

castAudition.name: Theo
castAudition.note: he adds energy and volume to the room but doesn't embody the core act of making space for others.

castAudition.name: Wren
castAudition.note: her observational stance positions her as a recipient of the welcome, not its initiator.

the-room-fills.whyWon: Dee's specific action of saving a seat is the most direct and active embodiment of the genre's 'found-family' promise, creating belonging rather than just inhabiting it.

a-rough-one.castPick: Wren — her 'sees everything' nature makes her the ideal 'Witness'; she offers support not through words but through the potent, non-intrusive act of 'Shared-Stillness'.
a-rough-one.premiseHoned: Wren notices the usual spark in Theo is gone, replaced by a tense quietness as he stares at a crumpled test paper. She doesn't ask what's wrong. She just pulls her sketchbook to the same table and begins to draw, her silent presence a steady anchor next to his storm.
a-rough-one.review: A powerful, non-verbal scene that showcases deep peer support without resorting to dialogue.

castAudition.name: The Caretaker
castAudition.note: his direct intervention would violate the genre rule against an 'adult-savior' resolving teen problems.

castAudition.name: Dee
castAudition.note: she would offer direct verbal support, which fits the culture lens but could feel prescriptive.

castAudition.name: Theo
castAudition.note: he would use humor to deflect the tension, a realistic but less emotionally resonant approach.

castAudition.name: Wren
castAudition.note: her quiet, observant empathy allows for a subtle, non-performative act of genuine support.

a-rough-one.whyWon: Wren's quiet, observant nature facilitates support through a 'small real detail' — a non-verbal gesture that feels more authentic and less like a moral lesson than overt dialogue would.

it-settles.castPick: Theo — emerging from a moment of vulnerability, his re-entry as a 'Collaborator' via a 'Gentle-Task' provides a low-stakes path back to the group's warmth.
it-settles.premiseHoned: The heavy mood has lifted, leaving a fragile quiet. On the whiteboard, a silly, sprawling doodle has begun. Wren wordlessly holds out a marker to Theo. It's not a demand to be funny or loud, just a simple invitation to add a line, to be part of something small and shared again.
it-settles.review: Beautifully visualizes the process of emotional recovery and gentle re-integration within a group.

castAudition.name: The Caretaker
castAudition.note: his presence is ambient and foundational, not the active social energy of the settled group.

castAudition.name: Dee
castAudition.note: her focus on one-to-one connection is part of the scene, but not its central energetic driver.

castAudition.name: Theo
castAudition.note: his performative humor effectively resets the group's energy to a warm, positive baseline.

castAudition.name: Wren
castAudition.note: her quiet contentment perfectly reflects the settled mood but doesn't actively create it for the group.

it-settles.whyWon: Theo's humor is the ideal catalyst to shift the group's energy after a tense moment, embodying the 'peer JOY' and higher-arousal positive state valorized by the culture lens.

the-walk-out.castPick: Dee — as a core member, she is the perfect 'Integrator' to carry the feeling of the room outwards, framing the episode's end with a 'Reflective-Coda'.
the-walk-out.premiseHoned: Walking home, the evening air is cool, but Dee feels the warmth of the room still clinging to her. The day's anxieties haven't vanished, but they feel smaller, buffered by the unconditional acceptance she just left. She is walking alone, but she is not lonely.
the-walk-out.review: Provides a resonant, hopeful closing note that emphasizes the lasting impact of the safe space.

castAudition.name: The Caretaker
castAudition.note: his quiet acknowledgment provides a warm bookend but lacks the peer-to-peer emotional core.

castAudition.name: Dee
castAudition.note: her role as social anchor naturally extends to ensuring the group's connection persists after they leave.

castAudition.name: Theo
castAudition.note: his loud exit provides a final burst of energy but is less focused on future connection.

castAudition.name: Wren
castAudition.note: her departure would be quiet and internal, not driving the scene's social conclusion.

the-walk-out.whyWon: Dee is best positioned to carry the found-family's warmth beyond the physical room, reinforcing the social bonds and the promise of continuity that is core to the genre.

rangeCandidates.name: The Open Seat
rangeCandidates.throughline: belonging you don't audition for — the chair's yours before you've proven anything; you're kept, not chosen
env: a free chair by the window, no name on it
the room fills; nobody checks your worth at the door
someone's sure they don't belong — the seat holds anyway
you sit at your lowest and it holds, unconditional
you leave having simply been somewhere that kept you
rangeCandidates.audienceServe: Speaks to low self-worth and the comparison spiral of not being 'enough' to belong.
rangeCandidates.wbRead: Frames a story about a character learning to accept unearned grace, externalized by the ever-present chair.

rangeCandidates.name: The Last Bell
rangeCandidates.throughline: the day ends whether or not you finished — the bell closes the hour; showing up IS the win, the unstarted is allowed to stay unstarted
env: the last bell, the bus skipped, the room instead
the hour deepens, the homework still half-begun
the spike — nothing's done, you're behind, you're lazy
the half-done thing set down, the kettle clicking off
the keys rattle: the hour ends, finished or not, and that's allowed
rangeCandidates.audienceServe: Speaks directly to ADHD-related school-shame and the pressure of the unfinished.
rangeCandidates.wbRead: Frames a story about a character whose worth is tied to productivity, finding relief in a space where 'done' is not the goal.

rangeCandidates.name: The Circle
rangeCandidates.throughline: no audition to belong — the un-evaluated hour; nobody is grading you, the performance treadmill stops at the door
env: the door shuts on the perceived, performed day
the circle, nobody performing okay at anyone
the loud mask cracks and nobody points at it
you stop doing the math on whether you belong
you walk out un-graded, lighter, having proved nothing
rangeCandidates.audienceServe: Speaks to social anxiety and the exhausting performance treadmill of being perceived.
rangeCandidates.wbRead: Frames a story about a character terrified of their social 'mask' cracking, and the freedom that comes after.

worldbuild.note: The richest metaphors are structural: healing comes from how the world itself is bounded (by time, by space), not just from the people in it.

culture.name: Western (Anglo-contemporary)
culture.valorises: higher-arousal positive states — excitement, expressiveness (HAP), within the cozy floor
culture.expression: direct, named feelings, spoken warmth, the explicit gesture
culture.nudge: toward a touch more directness + expressed emotion (still calm-floored) — the said over the merely implied

genre.name: contemporary YA slice-of-life (the after-school found-family)
genre.honour: the FOUND-FAMILY of misfits · a contemporary, un-condescending teen voice · stakes that are SOCIAL + emotional (a rupture, being left-on-read, fitting in) · the third-place hangout as the heart · the small real detail over the stated lesson
genre.traps: the 'adult's idea of teens' (stilted slang, moralising) · the after-school-SPECIAL lesson tacked on · sanitising the mess · an adult-savior resolving the teens' world
